title: A Code LM Interpretability Sandbox
summary: A from-scratch code LM built as a substrate for mechanistic interpretability experimentation
subtitle: A small code LM I'm training from scratch, used as a substrate for mechanistic interpretability experiments - circuit tracing, activation patching, attention attribution, and applied experimentation.

heroImage.alt: Training loss, LR schedule, and gradient norm over 3000 iterations

finding.body: Most interpretability work bolts hooks onto a model someone else trained. Ariadne goes the other way: a small code LM built from scratch, with structural choices made so the residual stream stays readable later.

sections.body: The training pipeline is working end-to-end - corpus, tokenizer, model, training loop. Loss curves below are from a recent run. Next is scaling the model into the 250-300M parameter range, then layering MI tooling on top: circuit tracing, activation patching, attention attribution.

sections.title: Where it stands
sections.body: GPT-2-style decoder: pre-LN blocks, causal self-attention, weight tying between embedding and output, scaled init. Standard modern training stack - bf16 autocast, fused AdamW, `torch.compile`, gradient accumulation, cosine LR with linear warmup. Loss curves are clean and the gradient norm settles below 1 after warmup, which is what I want before scaling.

image.alt: Training loss, LR schedule, and gradient norm over 3000 iterations

sections.title: Semi-supervised BPE
sections.intro: The tokenizer is byte-level BPE with two non-standard additions. Without these, BPE will happily split `const` into `con` + `st` because `con` is a common English prefix, and the model loses the concept of `const` as a unit.
items: **Locked Python keywords get seeded before merging.** Control flow (`if`, `else`, `for`, `while`, `return`, `yield`, `break`), definitions (`def`, `class`, `lambda`, `import`, `from`, `try`, `except`), identity (`self`, `None`, `True`, `False`), logic (`and`, `or`, `not`, `is`, `in`), and comparison/assignment operators (`==`, `!=`, `<=`, `>=`, `+=`, `-=`, `->`). These get prefixed onto the BPE split regex, so they're matched and emitted before frequency-based merging ever sees them. Pre-seeding them into the encoder cache at init also means the most common Python tokens are always cache hits.

**Scope is a token, not whitespace.** `<|indent|>` and `<|dedent|>` replace 2/4-space or tab runs with explicit scope-transition tokens, which is what Python's own lexer does. A deeply nested block costs one token per scope level instead of 16 spaces, and tab width is detected per document so 2-space and 4-space samples both compress correctly.

**FIM is wired in at 40%.** Forty percent of code samples get wrapped with `<|fim_prefix|>` / `<|fim_middle|>` / `<|fim_suffix|>` sentinels around a random split point, with a sub-fraction reordered as suffix-prefix-middle. This trains infill (bug-fix shaped) alongside left-to-right generation.

**Why this matters for the interp work.** Structural tokens make scope transitions and edit boundaries first-class objects in the residual stream. That's where I want to attribute attention heads against later, and you can't attribute against something the tokenizer threw away.

sections.title: Where it's headed
items: **Scale up.** the model to ~250-300M parameters with FlashAttention enabled (or as high as an H100 let's us go)
**Interpretability hooks.** Activation patching, attention attribution, circuit tracing - the standard mech interp toolkit, applied to a model where I know what every structural decision was for.

**Sandbox layout.** Reorganize the repo so each technique is a notebook over a fixed checkpoint rather than a one-off. The point is to make running new MI experiments cheap once the base model is frozen.
